---
title: "Exercise: Joining two tables from Rust"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

Take the full `strain` + `assay` database and answer two real questions from a Rust binary — one filtered, one aggregated:

```rust
fn assays_for_species(conn: &Connection, species: &str) -> Result<Vec<AssayRow>>;
fn species_summary(conn:  &Connection)                  -> Result<Vec<SpeciesSummary>>;
```

This is the day-7 capstone. It pulls together everything: a [`JOIN`](https://www.sqlite.org/syntax/join-operator.html) across two tables (exercise 02), [`Connection::prepare`](https://docs.rs/rusqlite/latest/rusqlite/struct.Connection.html#method.prepare) + [`query_map`](https://docs.rs/rusqlite/latest/rusqlite/struct.Statement.html#method.query_map) (exercise 03), and the `LEFT JOIN` + [`GROUP BY`](https://www.sqlite.org/lang_select.html#groupby) shape you need so that species with zero recorded assays still appear.

## The exercise

```bash
cd day7/ex-strain-query
```

Starter contents:

- `Cargo.toml` with `rusqlite = { version = "0.31", features = ["bundled"] }`.
- `src/main.rs` with the two `Result`-returning functions, two output structs, a `main` that dispatches based on whether a species was given on the CLI, and five tests in `mod tests`.
- `data/seed.sql` — same as exercises 02 and 03.

Implement both functions until five tests pass.

## How to run

```bash
rm -f data/strains.db
sqlite3 data/strains.db < data/seed.sql

cargo test

cargo run -- data/strains.db                          # one row per species
cargo run -- data/strains.db "Escherichia coli"       # every E. coli assay
```

Expected (truncated):

```
species                      n_strains n_assays  mean_od
Bacillus subtilis                    1        2     1.38
Escherichia coli                     4       11     1.42
Pseudomonas aeruginosa               1        2     1.41
Salmonella enterica                  1        2     1.25
Staphylococcus aureus                2        5     1.44
Streptococcus mutans                 1        2     1.20
```

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 — `assays_for_species` is exercise 03 + a JOIN"}
The shape is exactly the same as `strains_of_species` from yesterday, but the SELECT pulls columns from two tables:

```sql
SELECT s.strain_name, s.species, a.medium, a.od600_24h, a.date_measured
FROM strain AS s
JOIN assay  AS a ON a.strain_id = s.strain_id
WHERE s.species = ?1
ORDER BY s.strain_name, a.date_measured
```

The Rust side is identical to exercise 03 — five [`row.get(i)`](https://docs.rs/rusqlite/latest/rusqlite/struct.Row.html#method.get) calls in order. The first three columns are `NOT NULL` (`String`); the last two are nullable (`Option<f64>`, `Option<String>`).
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 — `species_summary` needs `LEFT JOIN`"}
If you use `INNER JOIN`, species that have zero recorded assays *vanish from the output*. The test `summary_handles_species_with_no_assays` is exactly that case — it inserts a Vibrio strain with no assays and checks it still appears with `n_assays = 0` and `mean_od = None`.

`LEFT JOIN` keeps every row from the left table (`strain`), filling `NULL` on the right where there is no match. [`AVG`](https://www.sqlite.org/lang_aggfunc.html#avg) over zero non-NULL values returns SQL `NULL`, which `rusqlite` reads into `Option<f64>::None`. So your SQL is:

```sql
SELECT s.species,
       COUNT(DISTINCT s.strain_id) AS n_strains,
       COUNT(a.assay_id)           AS n_assays,
       AVG(a.od600_24h)            AS mean_od
FROM strain AS s
LEFT JOIN assay AS a ON a.strain_id = s.strain_id
GROUP BY s.species
ORDER BY s.species
```

Note `COUNT(a.assay_id)` rather than `COUNT(*)` — [`COUNT`](https://www.sqlite.org/lang_aggfunc.html#count) of a column ignores NULLs, which is what makes a species with no assays come out as `n_assays = 0` instead of `1`.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 3 — mapping the summary row"}
```rust
Ok(SpeciesSummary {
    species:   row.get(0)?,   // String
    n_strains: row.get(1)?,   // i64
    n_assays:  row.get(2)?,   // i64
    mean_od:   row.get(3)?,   // Option<f64>  — AVG can be NULL
})
```

The single most common mistake here is declaring `mean_od: f64`. The first species with no assays will crash at runtime with `InvalidColumnType`. The test catches it.
:::

## Solution

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```rust
pub fn assays_for_species(conn: &Connection, species: &str) -> Result<Vec<AssayRow>> {
    let mut stmt = conn.prepare(
        "SELECT s.strain_name, s.species, a.medium, a.od600_24h, a.date_measured
         FROM strain AS s
         JOIN assay  AS a ON a.strain_id = s.strain_id
         WHERE s.species = ?1
         ORDER BY s.strain_name, a.date_measured",
    )?;
    let rows = stmt.query_map([species], |row| {
        Ok(AssayRow {
            strain_name:   row.get(0)?,
            species:       row.get(1)?,
            medium:        row.get(2)?,
            od600_24h:     row.get(3)?,
            date_measured: row.get(4)?,
        })
    })?;
    rows.collect()
}

pub fn species_summary(conn: &Connection) -> Result<Vec<SpeciesSummary>> {
    let mut stmt = conn.prepare(
        "SELECT s.species,
                COUNT(DISTINCT s.strain_id) AS n_strains,
                COUNT(a.assay_id)           AS n_assays,
                AVG(a.od600_24h)            AS mean_od
         FROM strain AS s
         LEFT JOIN assay AS a ON a.strain_id = s.strain_id
         GROUP BY s.species
         ORDER BY s.species",
    )?;
    let rows = stmt.query_map([], |row| {
        Ok(SpeciesSummary {
            species:   row.get(0)?,
            n_strains: row.get(1)?,
            n_assays:  row.get(2)?,
            mean_od:   row.get(3)?,
        })
    })?;
    rows.collect()
}
```

Full reference: `day7/ex-strain-query-solution/src/main.rs`.
:::

## Where day 7 ends

You can now:

- Design a small relational schema with a primary key, a foreign key, and the right `NOT NULL` constraints.
- Drive it from the `sqlite3` CLI for one-off exploration.
- Read and write the same file from Rust without ever leaving the type system.
- Distinguish `INNER` from `LEFT` joins — and know why the choice matters.

That covers the 80% of database work that actually fills a working day. The 20% you have not seen — indexes, transactions across HTTP calls, schema migrations, the async ecosystem — all build on these same primitives.

## What we never had time for

A small reading list, in increasing order of "you might need this":

- [**`UPDATE`** and **`DELETE`**](https://www.sqlite.org/lang.html) — modify existing rows. Same `?1` parameter pattern as `INSERT`.
- [**Indexes**](https://www.sqlite.org/lang_createindex.html) — `CREATE INDEX ix_strain_species ON strain(species);` turns a `WHERE species = ?` filter from O(n) to O(log n). You will notice the moment one table has 10⁶ rows.
- [**`EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN`**](https://www.sqlite.org/eqp.html) — paste it in front of any `SELECT` to see which indexes SQLite is using.
- [**Migrations**](https://docs.rs/refinery/) (`refinery`, `sqlx-cli`) — version-controlled `ALTER TABLE` so a schema change rolls forward in production safely.
- [**`sqlx`**](https://docs.rs/sqlx/), [**`SeaORM`**](https://www.sea-ql.org/SeaORM/) — async SQL on top of Tokio. Same model you saw on day 6 with `axum`. SQLite, PostgreSQL, MySQL — pick at compile time.
- [**Designing Data-Intensive Applications**](https://dataintensive.net/) — the long-form sequel to today.
